text: The surgeon begins by taking down omental adhesions from the liver edge, working on the medial aspect of the gallbladder. The dissection progresses towards the fundus of the gallbladder on the lateral side. A small incision into the gallbladder reveals necrotic purulence and fibrinous material emanating from its lumen.

The surgeon continues to remove the omentum from the gallbladder, achieving full separation. The drain is removed to allow for better retraction of the liver, revealing numerous adhesions above the liver that are dissected for improved exposure. The peritoneum is opened on either side of the suspected cystic duct, while the common duct is visible.

The surgeon attempts to isolate the cystic duct, aiming to achieve a normal critical view despite the duct not lighting up with ICG due to obstruction. A small vessel is encountered during the dissection. The common bile duct is visible, positioned down and away from the surgical field. The surgeon begins to identify the junction between the gallbladder and the cystic duct, working to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed.

The surgeon encounters additional vessels near the suspected cystic duct and creates a window behind it. Despite the lack of ICG confirmation and inability to achieve a critical view, the surgeon attempts to peel the gallbladder from the liver bed. Unable to identify the artery, the surgeon transects the gallbladder one-third up, revealing that the bottom half lacks a lumen.

The surgeon identifies a solid structure believed to be the artery on the medial aspect of the gallbladder. Using a modified top-down approach, they attempt to create a window behind the bottom third of the gallbladder to better identify the cystic duct. Crossing vessels are observed superiorly, and the surgeon cautiously divides the gallbladder above these structures. Upon compressing the suspected cystic duct, mucus is seen returning, confirming the presence of a lumen, which is then clipped.

The surgeon divides the artery and proceeds to detach the fibrotic gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully removing the remaining attachments. The surgical field is irrigated, and hemostasis is ensured.

I did leave a drain. The patient was admitted for a couple of days more for their medical comorbidities than the surgical procedure. There's the common duct right there and what I believe to have been the cystic duct beneath there. Although a little unusual, I felt comfortable that I hadn't injured anything and I completed the case and the patient did well and I hope you enjoy.
